title: Directions & Places
shortSummary: Ask the way, understand directions, and name the places around an Italian town.
culturalNote: Italian towns are built around a central piazza, and locals often give directions in relation to landmarks like the church (la chiesa), the main square (la piazza), or the town hall (il municipio) rather than street names. Distances on foot are common, so expect answers like sempre dritto (straight ahead) and è qui vicino (it's nearby). People are generally happy to help and may even walk you part of the way. Knowing destra (right) and sinistra (left) and the verb girare (to turn) covers most of what you need.

keyPhrases.english: How do I get to the centre?
to the right of the bar

grammar.rule: Dov'è and the contraction with è
grammar.explanation: Dove (where) contracts to dov' before è (is), giving dov'è (where is). The same elision happens with com'è (how is) and cos'è (what is).
grammar.commonMistake: Writing dove è in full. Before è it must elide to dov'è.

grammar.explanation: When a stranger gives you directions formally, the verb takes the Lei command form, often ending in -i: giri (turn), vada (go), prenda (take). With a friend you would hear the tu form gira, va', prendi.
grammar.commonMistake: Mixing the tu and Lei command forms in the same polite exchange.

dialogue.setup: A visitor stops a local to find the museum.

lines.speaker: Visitatore
lines.english: Excuse me, where is the museum?

lines.speaker: Signora
lines.native: Poi giri a destra al semaforo.
lines.english: Then turn right at the traffic light.

lines.speaker: Signora
lines.native: No, è qui vicino, cinque minuti.

quiz.question: How do you say 'Where is the station?' in Italian?
quiz.explanation: Dov'è is the contracted form of dove è.

quiz.question: What does sempre dritto mean?
quiz.explanation: Sempre dritto means to keep going straight.

quiz.question: Giri a _____. (left)
quiz.explanation: Sinistra is left; destra is right.

quiz.question: Which phrase means 'I'm lost'?
quiz.explanation: Mi sono perso uses the reflexive verb perdersi.
